agenda_text: Regular Meeting Breakdown Procedural Items Closed Session (Executive Session) Receive Information Regarding District-Wide Intruder Detection Audit Report The board will privately receive results of a security audit examining how well the district detects potential intruders across its campuses. This session is closed to the public because Texas law allows school boards to discuss security measures and audits without public disclosure, to avoid compromising campus safety. Legal Update The board's attorney will provide a confidential briefing on any pending or anticipated legal matters involving the district. This is a routine closed-session item that allows the board to receive legal advice without publicly disclosing sensitive information. Conduct Level III Grievance Hearing A Level III grievance is the highest level of internal appeal a district employee or parent can file within CISD before seeking outside review. The board will privately hear this appeal as permitted by Texas law for personnel or student-related complaints. Discuss Principal Candidates The board will privately discuss candidates being considered for principal positions at several CISD campuses. Personnel deliberations are conducted in closed session under Texas law to protect the privacy of the individuals being considered. Action on Executive Session Items Consideration and Possible Action on Level III Grievance After the closed grievance hearing, the board will return to open session to vote on a resolution or decision regarding the Level III grievance. All final votes must occur in open session under Texas law. Special Recognition 2026 USA Co-ed Cheerleading National Champions — Caney Creek High School The Caney Creek High School co-ed cheerleading team will be recognized for winning a national championship in USA Cheer competition. This is a ceremonial honor with no board vote required. 2026 NCA Game Day Cheerleading National Champions — College Park High School The College Park High School cheerleading team will be recognized for winning a national championship in the NCA (National Cheerleaders Association) Game Day category. This is a ceremonial recognition. 2026 UIL Girls Wrestling State Champion — The Woodlands High School A student-athlete from The Woodlands High School will be honored for winning a UIL (University Interscholastic League) state championship in girls wrestling. This is a ceremonial recognition. 2026 UIL Boys and Girls Wrestling State Champions — College Park High School Student-athletes from College Park High School will be recognized for winning UIL state championships in both boys and girls wrestling. This is a ceremonial recognition. 2026 UIL Boys Freestyle Swimming State Champion — Oak Ridge High School A student-athlete from Oak Ridge High School will be honored for winning a UIL state championship in boys freestyle swimming. This is a ceremonial recognition. 2026 UIL Girls Team Swimming State Champions — The Woodlands High School The Woodlands High School girls swim team will be recognized for winning the UIL state team swimming championship. This is a ceremonial recognition. Consent Agenda Consider and Approve Minutes The board will approve the official written record of its previous meeting(s). This is a standard housekeeping item required to maintain an accurate public record. Consider and Approve Amendment to the Budget The board will consider a mid-year adjustment to the district's budget. Budget amendments are common throughout the school year as revenues and expenditures shift from original projections; board approval is required for any changes. Receive Human Resources Report and Consider and Approve Employment of Professional Personnel The board will receive a staffing update and vote to officially approve the hiring of professional staff (such as teachers or administrators). Board approval is required for employment of professional personnel under Texas law. Receive Financial Reports The board will receive routine financial reports showing the district's current revenue, expenditures, and overall fiscal position. This is an informational item required for board oversight of district finances. Consider and Approve Texas Teacher Evaluation and Support System (T-TESS) Appraisers T-TESS is the state's official framework for evaluating teacher performance. The board must formally approve the list of staff members who are certified and authorized to conduct teacher evaluations under this system. Request Ratification of the District's 2026-2027 Certification of Provision of Instructional Materials Form Texas requires school districts to certify annually that they are providing students with required instructional materials. The board will formally ratify this certification for the upcoming 2026-2027 school year. Consider Authorizing the District to Enter into an Interlocal Agreement with Region 16 Education Service Center and Interlocal Agreement with ESC 16's Sponsored Cooperative TexBuy This item authorizes CISD to participate in a purchasing cooperative (TexBuy) managed through Region 16 Education Service Center. Cooperative purchasing agreements allow the district to buy goods and services at pre-negotiated prices, which can reduce costs and administrative burden. Human Resources Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Bradley Elementary The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead Bradley Elementary School. Principal appointments are significant personnel decisions that require board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Suchma Elementary The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead Suchma Elementary School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Cox Intermediate The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead Cox Intermediate School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Travis Intermediate School The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead Travis Intermediate School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Stockton Junior High School The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead Stockton Junior High School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for York Junior High School The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead York Junior High School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Grand Oaks High School 9th The board will vote on appointing a principal to lead the 9th grade campus at Grand Oaks High School. This is a personnel action requiring board approval. Administration Receive Information and Approve the Campus Mascot for Kacy Arnold Elementary The board will officially select and approve a mascot for Kacy Arnold Elementary, which appears to be a newer or recently named CISD campus. The mascot is an important part of a school's identity and community culture. Receive Information and Approve the Campus Mascot for Lynn Cartwright Junior High School The board will officially select and approve a mascot for Lynn Cartwright Junior High School, another campus that likely requires a new identity as a newly established or named school. Receive District and Campus Future Enrollment Projection Information from Zonda Demographics The board will hear a presentation from Zonda Demographics, a professional firm, on projected future student enrollment across CISD campuses. Enrollment projections are used to plan staffing, facility needs, and budgeting. Discuss and Possibly Consider Local School Library Advisory Council (LSLAC) Texas law allows school districts to establish Local School Library Advisory Councils to provide input on library materials and policies. The board will discuss the formation or status of this council for CISD. Discussion of Commemorative Months and Other Issues Under School Board Policy EMB The board will discuss how the district recognizes or observes commemorative months (such as heritage or awareness months) and related matters as governed by district policy EMB. This may involve reviewing or clarifying current policy on how these observances are handled in schools. Teaching and Learning Consider and Possibly Approve Library Materials in Accordance with Texas Education Code §33.026, School Board Policy EFB, and Senate Bill 13 The board will vote on approving specific materials for inclusion in CISD school libraries, following requirements set by Texas law (including Senate Bill 13, which established new rules for library material review). This item involves decisions about what books and resources are available to students in district libraries. Receive Information Regarding the Beginning and Middle of Year Northwest Evaluation Association Measures of Academic Progress Growth and Reading Fluency Assessment, House Bill 3 Progress Measure 1.2 and 2.2, and Bluebonnet Learning ELAR K-5 The board will receive a data report on how CISD students are performing on several academic assessments, including NWEA MAP (a widely used standardized test measuring academic growth), reading fluency scores, and state-required literacy metrics tied to House Bill 3. This is an informational presentation to keep the board updated on student academic progress. Business/Finance Receive Personnel Audit Presentation by the Docentus Group The Docentus Group, an outside consulting firm, will present findings from a personnel audit of the district. Personnel audits examine staffing structures, compensation, and workforce efficiency, and are typically used to help the district make informed decisions about human resources and budgeting. Planning and Construction Consider and Approve the Guaranteed Maximum Price Amendment for the Irons, Peet, York, and Conroe High School 9th Renovations Project and Authorize the Superintendent to Complete Negotiations and Execute the Contract Documents The board will vote to set a Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP) — a construction contract term that caps the total cost the district will pay — for renovation projects at Irons, Peet, and York Junior Highs and Conroe High School's 9th grade campus. This is a significant spending decision that also authorizes the Superintendent to finalize and sign the contracts. Consider and Accept as Complete the Armstrong, Austin, Birnham Woods, and Broadway Elementary Renovations Project The board will formally vote to accept renovation work at four elementary campuses — Armstrong, Austin, Birnham Woods, and Broadway — as fully complete. Official board acceptance is required to close out construction contracts and release any remaining contractor payments. Consider and Accept as Complete Conroe High School Tennis Courts at the 9th Grade Campus Project The board will vote to officially accept the construction of tennis courts at Conroe High School's 9th grade campus as complete. As with other completed projects, this formal acceptance closes out the construction contract.

Part 1: Public Hearing — Montgomery Trace Section 4 Detachment/Annexation Petition Purpose of Hearing The Board convened a public hearing pursuant to Texas Education Code §§13.03 and 13.051 to consider a petition seeking to detach Montgomery Trace Section 4 from Conroe ISD and annex it to Montgomery ISD. Citizen Participation No members of the public signed up to speak during the public comment period. Petitioner Presentation — Wisdom Idollet Resident, Montgomery Trace Section 4 Mr. Idollet presented on behalf of the neighborhood. Key points: - Neighborhood profile: Approximately 80 homes; borders Montgomery ISD to the north and west; accessible only via Hempie Egypt Road, which is otherwise entirely within Montgomery ISD. - Geographic anomaly: The CISD boundary appears to have crossed a creek specifically to include this neighborhood, which Mr. Idollet described as historically unusual. - Bus routing: CISD buses must travel 4–8 extra miles through other districts to serve the neighborhood. - Petition signatures: 53% of registered voters signed meeting the TEC majority threshold ; approximately 63% of actual current residents signed. About 12% were never contacted. - Reasons cited by residents for wanting to transfer: - Montgomery ISD schools are closer on average - Most residents already socialize, shop, and attend church in Montgomery - Perceived safety advantage near Montgomery schools based on CrimeGrades.org - Higher proficiency/SAT scores at Montgomery schools they would be zoned to - Bus routing inefficiency for CISD Challenges acknowledged by Mr. Idollet: 1. Property value ratio: The neighborhood falls approximately 4 students short of meeting the TEC ratio requirement. He noted that a majority of CISD board members signing the petition could satisfy this requirement under TEC. 2. Meets and bounds description: CISD legal counsel found the petition's legal description inadequate. Mr. Idollet acknowledged the petitioners are not lawyers or surveyors and worked from the neighborhood plat. 3. Debt transfer: Montgomery ISD expressed concern about inheriting CISD debt associated with the territory. Board Questions for Mr. Idollet - Trustee unidentified : Asked whether residents were aware of the tax rate increase. Mr. Idollet confirmed all signatories were informed; average annual increase would be approximately $568 per household . Most were agreeable; only 2–3 families objected on those grounds. Residents over 65 with locked-in taxes were generally supportive. - Trustee: Asked about Montgomery ISD's open enrollment policy. Mr. Idollet clarified that Montgomery ISD closed its full open enrollment 1–2 years ago and now has a limited, application-based process. He stated the decision before the board was partly premised on open enrollment still being available, which he said is no longer the case. - Trustee: Asked how many K–12 children are in the neighborhood. Mr. Idollet estimated 22 currently enrolled in CISD , with some already transferred to Montgomery ISD before open enrollment closed, and some homeschooling families. - Trustee: Asked about the historical reason the boundary crossed the creek. No one present had that information. District Presentation — General Counsel Key points presented on behalf of CISD: - Montgomery ISD voted against the annexation , citing concerns about inheriting CISD debt and other issues. - Tax rate impact: CISD's tax rate is currently lower than Montgomery ISD's. The average home in the area is valued at approximately $420,000, resulting in an estimated $568/year increase per homeowner if annexed. - Assessed value of territory: Approximately $50 million assessed; approximately $38 million taxable after exemptions . - 94 total parcels in the subdivision per Montgomery Central Appraisal District records. - 26 students were identified as currently riding CISD buses from the subdivision this school year; however, the statute requires using the prior year's last day of school enrollment figure — 22 students — for the ratio calculation. - Statutory ratio requirement TEC Chapter 13 : The ratio of taxable property value of the territory to total CISD taxable value must not exceed the ratio of school-age children in the territory to total CISD enrollment. In this case, the property value ratio is higher than the student ratio , meaning the statutory threshold is not met and the board lacks authority to proceed. - Montgomery ISD transfer option: General Counsel noted Montgomery ISD appears to still have a transfer application online. Mr. Idollet disputed this, stating full open enrollment has ended and the current process is limited and application-based. - Debt transfer question: A trustee asked whether CISD could simply retain the debt rather than transfer it. General Counsel was researching the statute in real time and could not give a firm answer. Staff noted that if CISD retains the debt but loses the taxable value and ADA, it would face a "double whammy" — losing both revenue streams while retaining the obligation. Board Discussion - Trustees discussed the historical oddity of the boundary crossing the creek and noted CISD has similar situations elsewhere e.g., buses traveling through Splendora ISD to reach Candy Creek area . - Trustees noted concern that the open enrollment premise cited in the district's presentation may be inaccurate based on Mr. Idollet's information. - One trustee highlighted the need for a firm legal answer on whether the debt must transfer before any future action could be considered. Motion and Vote Motion made by Board President : The board determine that the petition filed by Wisdom Idollet seeking detachment of Montgomery Trace Section 4 from Conroe ISD and annexation to Montgomery ISD fails to satisfy the mandatory property value ratio requirement set forth in Texas Education Code Chapter 13, including §§13.03 and 13.051, in that the ratio of taxable property value of the territory proposed to be detached to the taxable property value of the affected district does not meet the statutory threshold; and further that the board adopt the findings of fact and resolution reflecting that determination and decline to proceed with the requested detachment and annexation. Vote: 6–0 in favor motion carries Outcome: The petition was denied on statutory grounds. The hearing was formally concluded. Item 3: Walk-Through Weapons Detection Demonstration Overview Presented by: Mr. McCord, with input from Ethan and Chief The committee received information on three types of weapons detection systems, following up on discussion from the June meeting. Demonstrations were conducted at district facilities. 1. Garrett Walk-Through Metal Detectors Traditional Magnetometer - Currently in use at CISD stadiums - Cost: ~$6,000 per unit no ongoing subscription - Pros: Reasonably priced; no subscription fees; gives general location of detected item high/medium/low - Cons: Heavy; not easily deployable; finicky and requires constant interaction; long reset time between individuals slow throughput ; high false-positive rate; battery power unreliable needs AC ; nearly impossible to find repair technicians; best for indoor use 2. Evolve Machine Learning System - Cost: ~$70,000 for single-lane unit; ~$90,000 for dual-lane; ongoing software fee of $5,425–$7,500/year - Pros: Frictionless no reset time, two people can pass abreast simultaneously ; provides iPad with exact location indicator "red cube" on detected items; heavier/more stable in wind; handles outdoor use; machine learning improves over time - Cons: Very expensive; requires wired AC power; requires dedicated iPad monitor with high focus; heavy - Deployed at: NRG Stadium Texans games, Rodeo , Disney World, Minute Maid Park, The Cynthia Woods Mitchell Pavilion - CISD trial: Deployed at Woodforest Bank Stadium at a College Park football game 3. Open Gate Machine Learning System - Cost: ~$20,000+ per unit; no ongoing subscription optional extended warranty ~$1,600/year - Pros: Frictionless; easily transportable; battery powered uses large rechargeable batteries ; lighter weight; indoor/outdoor capable - Cons: Less stable in wind; battery-only no AC option ; does not provide location of detected item; less visually substantial - CISD trial: Experienced some wind issues during first outdoor deployment Key Takeaways from Discussion: - All systems require personnel to monitor gates plus an armed officer present at each screening point — the Chief strongly emphasized this as a critical safety requirement. - Sensitivity trade-off: Higher detection sensitivity catches more items but increases false positives and slows throughput. The "Holy Grail" is a setting just above the threshold where phones and keys would pass but dangerous items would be caught. - This is research, not purchasing — the district does not currently have allocated funds for these systems. - Potential future use: Home-base units at each high school for random checks; also deployable to stadiums and other campuses as needed. Daily screening of all students would be a significant community decision with structural building implications. - No system is perfect — even schools with daily weapons detection have experienced incidents. Camera-Based Detection Systems: - Previously researched discussed at June meeting ; companies quoted $7 million startup for one system and $400,000–$500,000/year for others. - Significant limitations identified: most systems required weapons to be brandished/visible not concealed ; did not detect edged weapons; limited range. - A committee member identified as a board member who attends Austin hearings requested the list of companies previously reviewed, noting she had seen demonstrations of systems capable of detecting concealed weapons. Staff agreed to provide this information. - A committee member asked about rental/lease/per-event options with third-party security companies providing equipment and personnel; staff noted evolve and open gate offer year-long leases without personnel but a per-event model would need further research. - Durability concerns were raised about transporting sensitive equipment between campuses.
